content.description:
A 2D spatial windowing environment that renders attachments as draggable, resizable windows. The primary container view for organizing multiple items visually. Position data lives in each child’s [attachment](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) `view` object — single source of truth, no separate layout table.

## Window Types

### Floating Windows
- Freely positioned via drag handle, corners to resize
- Click brings to front; window controls: minimize, maximize, close

### Pinned Windows (Corner-Anchored)
- Fixed to viewport corner (`top-right`, `bottom-left`, `bottom-right`)
- Stays in place relative to chosen corner on container resize

### Docked Windows (Edge-Anchored)
- Snapped to edge (`left`, `right`, `top`, `bottom`), fills perpendicular axis
- Single-axis resize on the non-docked edge

## Child Specification

Each child in `item.attachments` has a `view` object:

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| `x`, `y` | Number | Position in pixels (from anchor) |
| `width`, `height` | Number | Window dimensions |
| `z` | Number | Stored z-index (higher = front) |
| `anchor` | String/null | Positioning mode (see below) |
| `minimized` | Boolean | Minimized to pill bar |
| `maximized` | Boolean | Fills entire canvas |
| `type` | String/null | Optional view ID override |

### Position Interpretation

The meaning of `x` and `y` depends on the `anchor`:

| Anchor | x means | y means |
|--------|---------|----------|
| `null` / `top-left` | Distance from left edge | Distance from top edge |
| `top-right` | Distance from right edge | Distance from top edge |
| `bottom-left` | Distance from left edge | Distance from bottom edge |
| `bottom-right` | Distance from right edge | Distance from bottom edge |
| `left` / `right` | Distance from edge | (ignored - fills height) |
| `top` / `bottom` | (ignored - fills width) | Distance from edge |

## Window Chrome

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ ≡ │  Item Title             │ − □ × │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
  │    └── Drag handle           └── Window controls
  └── Menu button (pin/dock/unpin options)
```

- **Menu (≡)**: Pin, Dock Left/Right/Top/Bottom, Unpin/Undock
- **Minimize (−)**: Collapses to pill at bottom; **Maximize (□)**: Fills canvas
- **Close (×)**: Detaches child (floating only)
- **Pin indicator (📌)**: Shown on anchored windows; click to unpin/undock

## Z-Index Layering

The container uses two overlay layers without explicit z-index, so all children compete in the same stacking context:

| Layer | Z-Index | Contents |
|-------|---------|----------|
| Background | 0 | Inner view (in scrollArea) |
| Docked/pinned windows | 1 (ANCHOR_Z) | In dockOverlay (non-scrolling, pointer-events: none on container, auto on children) |
| Floating windows | 2+ (storedZ + baseZ) | In scrollArea (scrollable) |
| Maximized window | 1000000 | Covers everything |
| Minimized pills | 10000 | Direct children of container |
| Context menus | 100000 | Appended to document.body |

Z-index is DB-authoritative: `bringToFront` and `normalizeZIndices` read from the database only, never the DOM.

## Background Layer

Renders an optional inner view as full-bleed background beneath windows. The inner view bypasses cycle detection (deliberate composition, not a true cycle) and receives its own `getViewConfig()` scope. Change via View As > Spatial Canvas submenu.

## Sibling Container Protocol

Provides `siblingContainer.addSibling(itemId, navigateTo)` to child views:
- **New item**: Creates window at offset from last, brings to front
- **Existing item**: Brings to front, restores if minimized
- **navigateTo**: Optional anchor/line to scroll to after opening

Enables item links to open as new windows instead of navigating away.

## Scroll Preservation

Saves `scrollTop`/`scrollLeft` from all `.window-content` elements before re-render, restores after via double-`requestAnimationFrame`.

## Architecture

Decomposed into 5 focused libraries:
- [window-anchor-lib](item://2319aaaa-d991-43dc-8c04-724156f1ac18) — pure anchor positioning calculations
- [window-zindex-lib](item://bc80bdc2-f6b3-49df-a72d-1cecf866ddcf) — DB-authoritative z-index management
- [window-drag-resize-lib](item://9b319a24-ec36-4e5d-bf94-e7453febb380) — drag and resize handlers
- [window-minimize-lib](item://6dd254a8-dc94-4300-8a2e-19ce4165e22e) — single-path minimize/restore
- [window-menu-lib](item://f2bf33dc-3b41-4c9a-b0e5-62011eff239c) — window context menu

## Programmatic Examples

```javascript\n// Add a child window\nconst ws = await api.get('workspace-id');\nws.attachments.push({ id: 'item-id', view: { x: 100, y: 100, width: 600, height: 400, z: 10 } });\nawait api.update(ws);\n\n// Dock to left edge\nchild.view.anchor = 'left';\nchild.view.width = 300;\nawait api.update(ws);\n```

## Design Rationale

### Why Spatial Over Hierarchical?

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| **Spatial** | Multiple items visible, persistent layout, direct manipulation | More complex, position persistence needed |
| **Hierarchical** | Simpler, familiar tree navigation | Context switching, single focus, no spatial memory |

Spatial organization matches how people naturally arrange information. The complexity cost is worth the UX benefit for desktop workflows.

### Why Positioned Objects Over Separate Layout?

Position data could live in a separate layout table, but that creates synchronization issues (child removed but layout entry remains). Embedding position in the attachment `view` object is a single source of truth.

### Why Three Window Modes?

Windows support **floating** (freely positioned), **pinned** (corner-anchored), and **docked** (edge-anchored) modes. Floating covers general use. Pinned keeps reference panels visible during scrolling. Docked creates IDE-like panel layouts. All three use the same `anchor` field — null/unset for floating, corner string for pinned, edge string for docked.

### Why DB-Authoritative Z-Index?

Earlier versions read z-indices from both DOM and database, causing divergence and unbounded growth. The current design treats the database as the single source of truth — z-index functions read fresh attachments from the DB, never from DOM queries. This eliminates an entire class of stacking bugs.

### Mobile Strategy

The 2D canvas is desktop-focused. Mobile should use a different container view (e.g., linear list with swipe navigation). Different containers for different contexts is simpler than responsive design.

## Implementation Patterns

Spatial windowing relies on three critical patterns:

- ![Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — saves position without re-rendering
- ![Stale Closure Prevention](item://c0c0c0c0-0011-0000-0000-000000000000) — reads fresh data in handlers
- ![Scroll State Preservation](item://c0c0c0c0-0012-0000-0000-000000000000) — restores scroll across re-renders

Also see [Common Code Gotchas](item://c0c0c0c0-0016-0000-0000-000000000000) for parseInt zero trap and other pitfalls discovered during spatial windowing development.

## See Also

- [Attachments](item://c0c0c0c0-0005-0000-0000-000000000000) — The underlying data model
- [Content Field Conventions](item://c0c0c0c0-0024-0000-0000-000000000000) — Spatial field reference
- [Navigation](item://c0c0c0c0-0003-0000-0000-000000000000) — How links work in spatial context
- [viewport-rendering](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) — View dispatch and rendering
